planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist to dry, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; hairy alumroot is the most heat and drought-tolerant of the native heucheras and is excellent for difficult shaded sites in the Southeast.

directions: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist to dry, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; hairy alumroot is the most heat-tolerant of the eastern native heucheras and one of the few that thrives in the hot, humid summers of the Deep South. Heuchera villosa is a native southeastern US alumroot with large, maple-shaped, softly hairy leaves that may be green, bronze, or deeply burgundy-purple depending on cultivar, and produces airy, branched spikes of tiny, creamy-white flowers in late summer through fall — later blooming than most heucheras — making it a valuable late-season shade garden accent. It is notably deer-resistant, more tolerant of heavy clay soil than most heucheras, and is the parent of many popular modern shade garden cultivars; 'Autumn Bride' (white flowers, large leaves) and 'Caramel' (peachy-tan foliage) are outstanding selections for the South.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; American alumroot is an adaptable native groundcover and the progenitor of many modern coral bell cultivars.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; American alumroot adapts to a wide range of soil conditions including rocky slopes and is more tolerant of dry shade than many shade perennials. Heuchera americana is the wild-type species native to eastern North American rocky woods, slopes, and woodland borders from Ontario to Georgia; it produces mounded clumps of rounded, shallowly lobed, marbled or plain green leaves with attractive silver veining and metallic overtones that are the primary ornamental feature, while the slender, wiry scapes bearing tiny, inconspicuous, greenish-white flowers in late spring are secondary. The species is the primary parent of the huge number of foliage heuchera cultivars now available, though many of these are complex hybrids; the wild type is a fine native plant in its own right for naturalizing in shaded woodland gardens.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; coral bells is the species whose brilliant red flowers gave rise to the name and most early flower-heuchera hybrids.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; coral bells from the Southwest requires excellent drainage and is less tolerant of heavy clay or wet soils than eastern heuchera species. Heuchera sanguinea is a native of the Rocky Mountains and northern Mexico and is the species whose brilliant, bell-shaped, red to coral-red flowers on tall, wiry scapes gave rise to the common name "coral bells"; it produces rounded, scalloped, dark-green leaves with light-silver marbling and bears 12–18-inch flowering stems in late spring and early summer that are exceptional as cut flowers and irresistible to hummingbirds. Unlike the foliage heucheras derived from H. americana and H. villosa, H. sanguinea is grown primarily for its flower show; the named cultivars 'Firefly', 'Splendens', and 'White Cloud' are classic garden selections, and many modern landscape heucheras carry its flower genes.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in partial to full shade; Wherry's foamflower is a clumping, non-spreading species ideal where a well-behaved groundcover is needed without invasive spread.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in partial to full shade; Wherry's foamflower is a clumping species (unlike the stoloniferous T. cordifolia) and is better behaved in the garden without aggressive spreading, making it suitable for smaller shaded spaces. Tiarella wherryi is a native foamflower of the central and southern Appalachians producing deeply lobed, often bronze-veined, maple-like leaves in low mounds and slender spikes of small, delicate, white to pinkish flowers with prominent, thread-like stamens that give the flower spike its foamy appearance in mid-spring; the foliage often develops attractive burgundy or chocolate central veining or mottling that deepens in cool weather. It is an outstanding native groundcover for shaded rock gardens, woodland paths, and under-planted shrub borders; parent of many of the compact, non-running tiarella cultivars now popular in the trade.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in consistently moist to wet, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade along stream banks or at the margins of ponds; golden saxifrage requires consistent moisture and will not tolerate drought.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in consistently moist to wet, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade; alternate-leaved golden saxifrage forms dense, low, spreading mats along stream banks, seeps, and woodland spring margins and requires consistently high moisture — it will not survive drought. Chrysosplenium alternifolium is a diminutive, 3–6-inch-tall European and Asian native groundcover in the saxifrage family with small, rounded, kidney-shaped, toothed leaves and clusters of tiny, chartreuse-yellow to golden flowers surrounded by brightly colored, leaf-like bracts in early spring, creating a cheerful carpet of yellow-green along shaded, wet stream margins. It is uncommon in cultivation but is increasingly sought by shade and water garden enthusiasts for its ability to create a lush, moss-like carpet in the wettest, shadiest spots where other groundcovers fail; in North America it occurs natively in the far north.

planting.note: Plant divisions or seeds in fall in wet to saturated, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade near permanent water sources; skunk cabbage is a wetland obligate requiring consistently saturated soils.

directions: Plant divisions in fall in wet to saturated, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade along streams, pond margins, or in permanent swales; skunk cabbage is a wetland obligate and will die in drier conditions. Symplocarpus foetidus is one of the most extraordinary native plants of eastern North American wetlands; it is one of the very first plants to emerge in late winter, often pushing its mottled, burgundy-and-green, hooded spathe (resembling a bizarre tropical flower) through snow and ice in February — possible because thermogenic activity in the flower actually generates heat above freezing temperatures. The enormous, tropical-looking, glossy-green, cabbage-like leaves expand to 18–36 inches after the flower and create a bold, dramatic display at the water's edge through summer; when bruised, the plant emits a strong skunky odor that attracts carrion-fly pollinators. It is irreplaceable for bold foliage texture in wet woodland and bog gardens in zones 2–7.
